md: You already know two facts that look unrelated:
Both are special cases of one general formula. Here's how it works.

blocks.title: The interior-angle-sum formula
blocks.body:
For any convex polygon with $n$ sides:
$$\text{Sum of interior angles} = (n - 2) \cdot 180°$$

md: **Why?** Pick a vertex. Draw diagonals from that vertex to every non-adjacent vertex — this splits the polygon into $n - 2$ triangles. Each triangle's angles sum to $180°$, so the polygon's interior angles sum to $(n - 2) \cdot 180°$.
Use the slider below to step through different polygons. Watch the formula compute as $n$ changes.
### Each angle of a regular polygon
If the polygon is **regular** (all sides equal AND all angles equal), each angle equals the sum divided by $n$:

blocks.title: The exterior angle sum is always 360°
blocks.body: An **exterior angle** is the supplement of an interior angle (the angle you'd turn through as you walk around the boundary). For *any* convex polygon — whatever the value of $n$ — the **exterior angles always sum to exactly 360°**. That makes sense: walking once around the boundary is one full revolution.

blocks.tex: \text{Each exterior angle of a regular polygon} = \dfrac{360°}{n}

md: What is each interior angle of a regular 12-gon (dodecagon)?

Sum of interior angles: $(12 - 2) \cdot 180 = 1800°$. Each one: $\dfrac{1800}{12} = 150°$. (Or use the exterior trick: each exterior angle is $\dfrac{360}{12} = 30°$, so each interior is $180 - 30 = 150°$.)

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Interior-angle sum: $(n - 2) \cdot 180°$ for any convex polygon. Each interior angle of a regular $n$-gon: $(n - 2) \cdot 180 / n$. Exterior angles always sum to $360°$. These three facts cover almost every polygon angle question.
